data.title: First Ever White House Crypto Summit Kicks Off Today: Could New Crypto Like Best Wallet Token Benefit?
data.description:
The first-ever White House Crypto Summit takes place today, Friday, March 7. A bevy of industry powerplayers are participating, and while the agenda has yet to be announced, the conversation will undoubtedly include the National Crypto Reserve, the Digital Asset Stockpile, and other pro-crypto policies.

Trump has come a long way in his crypto journey, from crypto skeptic to now pledging to make the US the ‘Crypto Capital of the World’.

The executive order and summit meeting are good first steps in this direction. As sentiment around crypto rises in the US, could new crypto projects like Best Wallet Token ($BEST) cash in?

Crypto is the Strategic Order of the Day

The executive order establishing the Strategic Bitcoin Reserve and US Digital Asset Stockpile was officially issued on March 6 and sets clear expectations for all US government departments about pooling government-owned Bitcoin.

Departments have 30 days to audit and transfer any existing Bitcoin into the reserve. In 60 days, the Secretary of the Treasury will deliver considerations for establishing and managing the reserve and Digital Asset Stockpile.

A significant aspect of the order ensures that Bitcoin is held in reserve, demonstrating the value of it as a long-term investment. The order reaffirms sentiment to see crypto and digital assets the same way as traditional assets and harness their power rather than limit it.

Delivering on his pledge to ‘Make America the Crypto Capital of the World’, Trump has appointed David Sacks as Crypto Czar, who has already announced on X that no taxpayer dollars will be involved in the reserve. The reserve (estimated to be worth $17B in $BTC) has been seized from criminal activities.

Although the reserve refers explicitly to Bitcoin, Trump has shared on Truth Social (a Trump owned social media platform) he would look to include other prominent crypto such as Solana ($SOL) and Ethereum ($ETH).

These moves position the US as a effectively navigating the new economic waters — many countries haven’t adopted crypto as part of their reserves yet. Arguably, Trump’s actions display the inklings of a well-considered long-term strategy – one that balances the mandate to maintain dollar dominance with the impetus to embrace crypto.

Top Crypto Players Take Their Seat at the Table

Some of the biggest names from the crypto world are expected to attend today’s summit and discuss ways to open doors for the industry. Expected guests include:

  • Matt Huang: Co-founder of Paradigm
  • Sergey Nazarov: Co-founder of Chainlink
  • Caroline Pham: Acting chairman of the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission
  • JP Richardson: CEO of Exodus
  • David Sacks: AI and Crypto Czar
  • Michael Saylor: Founder of MicroStrategy
  • Arjun Sethi: CEO of Kraken
  • Vlad Tenev: CEO of Robinhood
  • Mark Uyeda: Acting chairman of the US Securities and Exchange Commission
  • Zach Witkoff: Co-founder of Trump-linked World Liberty Financial

It’s fair to assume the mood in the summit will be bullish, and with that comes a potential boost for the crypto markets. Could Ethereum-based wallet tokens, like Best Wallet Token, cash in on the increased attention?

Best Wallet Token to Benefit From Crypto Summit

So many key players from the world of crypto in one room means anything is possible, but it can’t fail to energize crypto markets. Altcoins like Best Wallet Token ($BEST) could ride the wave of exposure and investment.

In its presale, $BEST has raised $10.8M since launching in November 2024, and the current token cost is sitting at $0.02425, a 7.78% increase from the initial presale launch price. Dynamic staking rewards of 146% APY are still available to those who stake during the presale.

$BEST opens doors, just like today’s Summit should do for participants and the industry as a whole. Holders gain access to exclusive member perks within the Best Wallet ecosystem. These include early access to exciting new crypto projects and reduced transaction fees, higher staking gains, the upcoming Best Wallet debit card, and a Portfolio Management feature.

We’ll have to see what today’s crypto summit brings, but the exposure could cause Bitcoin and many of the best altcoins to shift upwards. Check out our guide on how to buy $BEST, and get in now.

Crypto is a volatile market; you can experience significant returns, but equally, you may lose your investment entirely. It’s important you do your own research before making any purchases.

data.title: Standard Chartered says funding of US Strategic Bitcoin Reserve could involve selling gold as a ‘budget-neutral’ option
data.description:
Standard Chartered’s Geoff Kendrick has highlighted a potential strategy for funding the newly established U.S. Strategic Bitcoin Reserve (SBR) in a budget-neutral manner — selling a portion of the country’s vast gold reserves to buy bitcoin.

“The next question is what could constitute budget-neutral strategies, and in theory, the following could be done, to sell gold and buy bitcoin," Kendrick said in an email. He added that the U.S. government holds $760 billion of gold.

This follows President Donald Trump’s overnight signing of an executive order officially establishing the Strategic Bitcoin Reserve and a Digital Asset Stockpile, marking a historic shift in the federal government’s approach to digital assets. The order mandates that any future government acquisitions of bitcoin for the reserve must follow budget-neutral strategies, ensuring no incremental costs to American taxpayers.

Under the new directive, all bitcoin currently held by the U.S. government will be transferred to the Strategic Bitcoin Reserve — with a clear stipulation that none of it will be sold.

Other potential budget-neutral strategies

Kendrick also suggested additional budget-neutral strategies that could be explored. One option, he said, is utilizing the Exchange Stabilization Fund (ESF), which holds $39 billion in net assets. While the ESF is traditionally used to stabilize liquidity during financial crises, Kendrick noted that repurposing it for bitcoin purchases “would be a clear change of direction.”

Another possibility is the Bitcoin Act 2024, introduced by Senator Cynthia Lummis, which proposes buying 200,000 bitcoin per year for five years. Kendrick suggested that, if structured appropriately, the Bitcoin Act “could be passed and worked into a budget-neutral way.”

President Trump is expected to expand on the Strategic Bitcoin Reserve during his remarks at the White House Digital Assets Summit on Friday. However, Kendrick cautioned that immediate policy implementations are unlikely, noting that Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent will play a critical role in shaping and executing the acquisition strategy. “In reality, I cannot see any of these three solutions being delivered today," Kendrick said. "Rather, they would all require Treasury Secretary Bessent to propose something, given the importance placed on Bessent in the executive order).

Global and market reactions

The Standard Chartered head of digital asset's research emphasized that the formalization of a U.S. Strategic Bitcoin Reserve could spur other sovereign nations to follow suit.

“I have written previously about how a U.S. strategic reserve, whatever it looks like, can embolden other sovereigns." Kendrick said. "At the end of December, Abu Dhabi held 4.7k BTC equivalent of IBIT. Other sovereigns will now surely join the buying.”

Kendrick also suggested that U.S. states and institutional investors, including long-term pension funds, could be encouraged to allocate to bitcoin following the federal government’s lead.

On market movements, Kendrick observed that bitcoin remains range-bound between $80,000 and $95,000, but expects an upside breakout. “While BTC remains in an 80-95k range, it is probably choppy, and there’s not a lot to do. But I think the next move is out the top of this range," he said. "Buy weekend dip caused by lack of news from Trump tonight, look for a break out of the top of this range soon, especially if the tariff noise can slow down for a while."

data.title: Intuitive Machines Stock Plunges Another 26% After Troublesome Lunar Landing — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Space is hard. That can make space-related stocks volatile.

Shares of Intuitive Machines dropped 26% to $8.36 on Friday, after falling 20% on Thursday.

The S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were up 0.3% and 0.2%, respectively.

Intuitive Machines shares fell Thursday after difficulties landing the company's Athena spaceship near the moon's south pole. The craft touched down around noon Eastern time, but NASA's broadcast of the landing finished without clear information about the status of the craft — whether it was upright and if all systems were operating as intended.

Intuitive CEO Steve Altemus said at a NASA press conference Thursday afternoon that he didn't believe Athena was upright.

"I think we can all agree ... that landing on the moon is extremely hard, " said Nicola Fox, NASA's associate director at the Science Missions Directorate. "Intuitive Machines was aiming to land in a place that humanity has not been before."

She added that the lander was returning data back from the moon.

Athena was the company's second landing. Its Odysseus lander touched down on the moon in February 2024. Shares were below $3 apiece early in 2024 and traded above $10 around the time of the landing. Shares ended the year at above $18 a share.

NASA called the Odysseus mission a success. It was the first time the U.S. had landed on the moon in 50-plus years. Odysseus, however, tipped over on landing.

Both landings were part of NASA's Commercial Lunar Payload Services program, which aims to have NASA contract for a payload delivery and then let commercial companies take care of delivery. In the past, NASA would partner with commercial companies for parts and equipment, but NASA would design and launch the ships.

"I'll use aviation as an example ... we don't build airplanes, we don't run airlines, but we are a customer and user of those," said NASA associate administrator Clayton Turner. "That's how you can imagine space going forward."

"Intuitive Machines will try again," wrote Benchmark analyst Josh Sullivan in a report Friday. "We view the move in Intuitive Machines stock as an overreaction given [company] backlog, and the context that space can often be an iteration process."

Sullivan rates shares Buy and has a $16 price target on the stock. Overall, seven of nine, or almost 80% of analysts covering shares, rate them Buy. The average analyst Buy-rating ratio for stocks in the S&P 500 is about 55%. The average analyst price target for Intuitive Machines' stock is about $18.

Despite the positive Wall Street sentiment, the after-hours plunge Thursday brought Intuitive Machines stock back to levels not seen since October 2024.

Investors need to brace for volatility when investing in space.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: Vandor Real Estate Socimi Obtains EUR117 Million Loan
data.description:
Vandor Real Estate Socimi secured a 117 million-euro loan from a banking syndicate, according to a Friday release.

The facilities have a five-year maturity, with an interest rate of three-month Euro Interbank Offered Rate plus an initial annual margin of 1.90%.

Upon signing of the facilities agreement, 75.9 million euros of the facility was drawn. Funds are earmarked for the real estate investor's refinancing of existing debt and for new asset acquisitions.

data.title: Adobe to Report Strong Q1 Results Ahead of Adobe Summit, RBC Says
data.description:
Adobe is expected to report strong Q1 results on March 12, ahead of the Adobe Summit the following week, RBC Capital Markets said in a note Friday.

Investor attention has mostly centered on Generative AI, creating concerns about competitive pressure as they await more details about GenAI monetization and Adobe Express growth strategies, the investment firm said.

Concerns over GenAI's timing and increased competition have pressured Adobe's stock in 2024, down 19% since Q4 results, compared with a 13% drop in megacap peers and a 6% drop in the S&P 500, RBC said.

Management maintains confidence in its GenAI roadmap but needs to show measurable outcomes with its new product launches, including Firefly Video in beta and additional Firefly plans, RBC analysts noted.

Adobe's job postings increased 15% quarter-over-quarter in Q1 after rising 20% in Q4, showing continued investment in company growth, according to the note.

RBC maintained an outperform rating on Adobe's stock but lowered the price target to $550 from $590.

data.title: Jobs Data Is a Mixed Bag for Investors — WSJ
data.description:
By Sam Goldfarb

Friday's jobs report has had a mixed impact on investors' bets on interest-rate cuts:

  • Traders now see a 72% chance the Federal Reserve will cut rates at least three times this year, interest-rate futures suggest, according to CME Group data. That's up from 66% on Thursday.
  • The chance the first cut happens by the Fed's May 7 meeting has dropped to 45%, from 52% Thursday.
  • That drop suggests the report was seen as strong enough to lessen the likelihood of the Fed moving quickly to cut rates again.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Canada Job Market 'Had Been Turning The Corner,' And Then Came a Trade War — Market Talk
data.description: Hiring stalls in Canada, based on February data, yet BMO Capital Markets chief economist Doug Porter believes the underlying details suggest some resilience--at least prior to tariff implementation. The unemployment rate was unchanged at 6.6%, or 5.6% using US Department of Labor methodology. Employment in retailing and wholesaling climbs by over 50,000, which Porter says indicate consumer strength. And wages edged upward. "It's reasonably clear that the job market had been turning the corner in recent months--until the trade war erupted," Porter writes. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

data.title: KLX Energy gains on debt refinancing plan
data.description:
** Shares of oilfield services firm KLX Energy rise ~24% to $5.12

** KLXE says it is refinancing its existing debt by issuing new $232 mln senior secured notes due in 2030

** Co also getting a new $125 mln credit facility to help with its financial operations

** Co has ~16.8 mln outstanding shares, with market cap of ~$69.2 mln - LSEG data

** KLXE says refinancing will extend the co's debt maturity profile

** Stock fell 55% in 2024

data.title: Jazz Pharma climbs after Morgan Stanley raises PT
data.description:
** Jazz Pharmaceuticals' shares rise 3% to $141.29

** Brokerage Morgan Stanley raises PT to $183 from $175, a 34.1% upside to the stock's last close; maintains "overweight" rating

** Says growth of Jazz's newer sleep disorder drug Xywav in H1 should offset ongoing switch from older drug Xyrem

** Sees 'meaningful expansion' opportunity for Jazz's breast cancer drug Ziihera

** Brokerage expects peak annual revenue of $240 million for brain tumor drug dordaviprone, Jazz's newly acquired drug from its buyout of Chimerix

** Six brokerages rate the stock "buy" or equivalent, one "neutral"; their median PT is $187.47, according to data compiled by LSEG

** JAZZ has risen 16.4% in the past 12 months

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0945 ET - Heightened uncertainty over U.S. trade policy starts to bleed into Canadian data, says TD Bank economist James Orlando, citing February's jobs report. Net employment was largely flat, with losses in manufacturing and logistics. "One month doesn't make a trend, but Canadians should be closely watching the labor market for signs of weakness in the months ahead," Orlando says, adding the job market is on relatively solid footing entering this fresh period of heightened tariff uncertainty. He expects a rate cut from Bank of Canada next week, as the central bank takes out insurance amid this turbulent time. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0843 ET - The euro's recent appreciation might not last given the threat of U.S. tariffs, Nordea analyst Philip Maldia Madsen says in a note. The euro's gains reflect the prospect of increased eurozone fiscal spending boosting the economy, he says. It's also supported by a weaker dollar as President Trump's tariffs and plans to reduce government spending dampen the economic outlook, he says. However, it's only a matter of time before Trump shifts his focus for tariffs over to Europe. "After the recent rally in the euro above $1.08, we believe the upside is limited and that a setback is likely in the coming months." The euro hits a four-month high of $1.0890, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0756 ET - Canada's auto industry likely won't face a prolonged tariff situation, according to TD Cowen. In a report, analyst Brian Morrison says that the recent implementation then reversal of tariffs shows the complexity of the auto supply chain and how prolonged tariff duration would be negative to the U.S.'s own auto industry and economy. "This is not the intent of the Trump administration, in our view, and as such we anticipate in time to see modest changes favouring a gradual shift to U.S. content with limited disruption by H2/25," he says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0629 ET - The dollar's weakness comes as the Trump administration's highly uncertain tariff policy appears to be damaging investor confidence and economic activity, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. U.K. investment slumped on uncertainty leading up to Brexit and "the same can be true of the U.S. where businesses and consumers just want some certainty and are not getting it so far." The dollar has also been hit by softer U.S. data and markets pricing in more Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts. The dollar could fall further if U.S. nonfarm payrolls data at 1330 GMT are softer, he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 103.752 after hitting a four-month low of 103.563 earlier. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0555 ET - The Canadian dollar has limited scope to rise after President Trump temporarily delayed tariffs on many goods from Canada and Mexico, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. "Markets had never priced in 25% tariffs as a long-lasting measure, and USD/CAD already corrected from the highs before yesterday's move." Canada also still faces a hit from reciprocal U.S. tariffs in April, he says. USD/CAD could rise to 1.44 in coming weeks, although a potentially softer U.S. nonfarm payrolls report at 1330 GMT could add some pressure on the exchange rate in the near term, he says. USD/CAD rises 0.1% to 1.4308 after hitting a one-and-a-half-week low of 1.4239 Thursday, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0415 ET - Bitcoin falls, even after President Trump signed an executive order Thursday to create a strategic bitcoin reserve and an additional stockpile of other digital assets. Cryptocurrencies are lower as the bitcoin reserve and stockpile will be capitalized by forfeited assets already owned by the government rather than by any new funding, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. The news came ahead of Friday's White House crypto summit. Bitcoin falls 1.3% to $88,237, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0355 ET - Oil prices rise in early trade but remain on track for a significant weekly loss amid fluctuating U.S. trade policies and OPEC+ output hike plans. Brent crude and WTI are both up 1.2% to $70.29 and $67.21 a barrel, respectively, after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the U.S. will intensify pressure on Iran and won't hesitate to go "all in" on sanctions on Russia if it helps in peace negotiations. Still, "oil markets are dealing with renewed uncertainty on the supply side and a darkening outlook on the demand side," analysts at Fitch's research unit BMI say. The U.S. partially pulled back tariffs on some goods from Mexico and Canada, adding further uncertainty over President Trump's trade strategy and its impact on global economic growth. Meanwhile, news that additional OPEC+ supply will soon hit the market is increasing downward pressure on prices. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1714 ET - The Trump administration says the one-month delay in implementing the 25% tariff on imports from Canada and Mexico will apply to goods considered compliant with USMCA, or the trade treaty. The talk of "USMCA compliant" triggers some head scratching among Canadian business leaders. "Most goods are covered by the trade treaty and Canadian business leaders are encouraged that the Trump Administration recognizes the importance of our trilateral trading pact," says Goldy Hyder, president of the Business Council of Canada, which represents blue-chip CEOs. "Any trade disputes should be worked out under the rules of the USMCA--that's why we have an agreement." (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1629 ET - Canada's largest business group, the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, says the Trump administration's latest reprieve on 25% tariffs, until April 2, should be no cause for celebration. "The economy is not a toy to play with. Constant threats and economic uncertainty have taken their toll," says Matthew Holmes, the chamber's executive vice president. Holmes says one way to end the heightened policy uncertainty is to move toward another renegotiation of USMCA, the North American trade treaty, that all countries will respect. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1355 ET - Uncertainty around the federal cost-cutting actions of DOGE is likely to cause private-sector employers to freeze hiring, adding to U.S. labor worries, Evercore ISI analysts write. They estimate that DOGE will directly eliminate a total of 200,000 jobs this year. Canceled government contracts and grants will subtract another nearly 250,000. The resulting paralysis among government contractors would eventually take the final tally to 500,000. The scenario assumes that uncertainties would last for a few months, "resolving in June with DOGE moving on to more structural review." Evercore ISI sees rising odds of a Fed cut in June stemming from DOGE actions. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1343 ET - Lawn-mower maker Toro has significantly reduced its exposure to China over the past few years, with the country currently accounting for a low-single-digit percent of its total cost of goods sold, CEO Rick Olson says on a call with analysts. Some of the company's residential and irrigation products are produced in Mexico, and it doesn't produce in Canada, he adds. Determining a gross impact from tariffs is a moving target right now, Olson says, though he adds that the company is "prepared to quickly make adjustments to our operations and pricing as appropriate." Toro backs its fiscal 2025 outlook, which includes the first round of Chinese tariffs and calls for sales to be flat to up 1% from last year. Toro falls 6.6% after logging lower profit in its F1Q. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1234 ET - Longer-term Treasury yields recover some ground as the Trump administration dials down the tariffs rhetoric, at lest temporarily. Trump posts on Truth Social that Mexican exports that fall under the USMCA agreement he negotiated in his first term won't be subject to tariffs until April 2. Commerce Secretary Lutnick suggested on CNBC that Canada could also get a reprieve. U.S. trade policy has become a major source of uncertainty roiling financial markets and boosting bets on interest rate cuts by the Fed. Treasury yields up to three years in maturity are falling, with the two-year at 3.985%. Longer-term ones rise, with the 10-year at 4.302%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Jobs Report Keeps Fed on Hold, Muddies Outlook for Cuts — WSJ
data.description:
By Nick Timiraos

Most Federal Reserve officials have said nothing to suggest they are ready to resume rate cuts this spring. February's payroll report did little to provide near-term urgency for lower rates.

The report showed a slight increase in the unemployment rate and other indications of softening demand for workers. It did little to dispel concerns in financial markets that economic weakness might accelerate in the coming weeks in a way that compels the Fed to cut interest rates in May or June.

Take, for example, a broad measure of underemployment that includes people who worked part time but would have preferred full-time work. This ticked up to 8% in February from 7.5% in January, reaching the highest level in more than three years.

The Fed is widely expected to hold interest rates steady at its meeting in two weeks. Expectations in interest-rate futures markets that the Fed might cut rates at its following meeting, in early May, edged lower after the employment report. But expectations for multiple rate cuts later in the year edged up.

Market sentiment about the economy has changed rapidly in the last three weeks. Reports about declining retail sales and consumer spending have added to concerns that disruptive steps by the Trump administration to escalate trade wars and shrink the federal workforce could chill economic hiring, investment and spending. A few weeks ago, investors thought the Fed might cut rates just once this year, but investors how expect at least three cuts this year.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0945 ET - Broadcom is facing plenty of headwinds, particularly from the Trump administration's trade war and potential limits on AI chip exports, but the company seems confident that its dominance on custom chip sales should keep it moving in the right direction, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. It has a growing base of elite custom ASIC chip customers, they say. Broadcom makes a compelling argument that application-custom chips for AI will continue to proliferate, including specific accelerators of aspects of training and inferencing, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0937 ET - MDA Space says that M&A is a big part of the company's expansion plans, opening up new markets and access to new talent. CEO Mike Greenley says on an analyst call that he has specific acquisition strategies: to be more vertically integrated and to secure the supply chain, and to add more capabilities in other geographic areas. The executive says the company is particularly focused on the US and Europe, where there is strong demand from governments for aerospace products. Another part of the consideration is access to talent in new geographies that could support the business. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0933 ET - MDA Space CEO Mike Greenley says the tariff situation is manageable for its business. On an analyst call, the executive says the aerospace company is actively engaged in dialogues with governments. "Tariffs come and tariffs go," the CEO says, but "when we look at our contracts, and we look at how tariffs work, we don't see a concern for us at this time." Part of the security comes from its customers, where about 90% of its business is from outside the U.S. The CEO also says the company's customers have the same perspective, and he notes that its new business pipeline remains "robust." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0926 ET - Broadcom delivered a series of forecast beats in its fiscal 1Q report, but perhaps the biggest surprise was the announcement that it is working with four new hyperscaler customers in designing custom silicon, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. The four are aiming to be just as big as the existing three, which are already scaling to 1 million units by 2027 with a serviceable addressable market that year of $60 billion to $90 billion, the Melius analysts say. The new customers won't contribute to revenue for another couple years, but the biggest one could be OpenAI with a contribution in 2026, they say. Broadcom climbs 7% premarket. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0911 ET - MDA Space says that most of its supply chain is based outside of the US. On an analyst call, executives of the Canadian aerospace company, say that only about one-quarter of its supply chain is sourced in the U.S., while the rest is part of a larger global network. The company is still keeping an eye on the evolving US tariff situation, but says that it hasn't felt a dampening of demand for its products and services. The company's backlog as of the end of the quarter was C$4.39 billion, up from C$3.1 billion a year earlier. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0858 ET - Hewlett Packard Enterprise's outlook is worrying as pricing discounts in its traditional server market compound with other challenges, say analysts at J.P. Morgan in a research note. The server and cloud-software company was already dealing with concerns about competition for AI servers and the impact of tariffs. Chief Financial Officer Marie Myers said pricing adjustments will be part of its mitigation strategy in light of the latest tariffs. "While the discounting-led headwinds the company is facing appear to be in some regards company-specific execution challenges... it raises concerns in relation to exacerbating an already aggressive pricing backdrop in the market which challenges profitability for not only HPE, but also peers," say the analystsShares tumble 17% to $14.82 premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0855 ET - Gold futures rise as gold exchange-traded funds report inflows in February. Futures are up 0.15% at $2,930.90 a troy ounce. Gold ETFs recorded strong inflows of 100 metric tons in February, the third consecutive month of inflows, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. This ETF demand helped propel gold to a record high of $2,974.0 an ounce in late February, Commerzbank says. The inflows were mainly concentrated in the U.S., where the largest inflow in a single month since July 2020, was recorded. European ETFs, on the other hand, only recorded a small increase in holdings. China saw ETF inflows of around 20 tons, or an increase of around 19%--indicating a growing interest in gold ETFs among Chinese investors. Until now, most Chinese investment demand was dominated by gold bars and coins, Commerzbank adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0827 ET - Oil's recent selloff might have been overdone in light of current market dynamics, according to SEB's chief commodities analyst, Bjarne Schieldrop. Brent crude and WTI both regained some ground on Friday, rising 1.4% higher to $70.46 and $67.30 a barrel, respectively. "The recent selloff was a little too much and a little too soon as [a] surplus is not here quite yet," Schieldrop says. Meanwhile, the price difference between Brent contracts for one month and three months ahead is widening. "The Brent one-month price is currently trading very soft versus the one-three month timespreads," according to the analyst. "So, more rebound is definitely possible given both time-spreads and technically oversold market." Still, crude futures are still on track for weekly losses of over 3% amid concerns over U.S. tariffs and OPEC+'s decision to start raising supply in April. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0756 ET - Canada's auto industry likely won't face a prolonged tariff situation, according to TD Cowen. In a report, analyst Brian Morrison says that the recent implementation then reversal of tariffs shows the complexity of the auto supply chain and how prolonged tariff duration would be negative to the U.S.'s own auto industry and economy. "This is not the intent of the Trump administration, in our view, and as such we anticipate in time to see modest changes favouring a gradual shift to U.S. content with limited disruption by H2/25," he says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0716 ET - Cleveland-Cliffs is launching a company-wide "Buy American" incentive for its nearly 30,000 employees. During calendar year 2025, any Cleveland-Cliffs employee who buys or leases a new American-built vehicle with substantial Cliffs' steel content will receive a $1,000 cash bonus in connection with the purchase. Vehicles produced in a plant outside of the U.S., or that use a significant amount of imported flat-rolled steel, will not qualify for the incentive. "We hope that other companies will follow suit and do their part to incentivize consumption of American-made products," says Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs' chairman and CEO. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0645 ET - TUI's first-quarter update is encouraging and concerns among some investors that holiday demand has plateaued are overdone, HSBC analysts Ali Naqvi and Joseph Thomas say in a research note. Current industry data suggests there remains good momentum for the summer, despite slower booking volumes and cost pressures, the analysts say. 82% of customers are planning to take holidays this year compared with 79% of survey respondents a year ago, the analysts say. "Operators like TUI are making smart decisions by focusing on high volume business and offsetting inflationary pressures by new product initiatives, including their growing mix of dynamically packaged holidays." The analysts raise their target price on TUI shares to 10.90 euros from 10.20 previously. Shares trade 0.69% lower to 7.15 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0635 ET - Regulation of artificial intelligence should focus on the application of the technology rather than the technology itself, Mistral AI Chief Executive Arthur Mensch said in an interview at the Mobile World Congress in Barcelona. "When you're building a medical diagnostic assistant, you need to make sure that it's accurate enough," Mensch said. However, regulating the models behind the technology itself "makes little sense in that it's more or less a programming language," he adds. Mensch said Mistral AI is engaging with the European Commission, the EU's executive arm, on the application of the EU AI Act. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: FTC Seeks More Info on Arthur J. Gallagher/AssuredPartners Deal — Update
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

Federal regulators are taking a closer look at Arthur J. Gallagher's planned $13.45 billion acquisition of insurance broker AssuredPartners.

Gallagher on Friday said the Federal Trade Commission has requested additional information as the antitrust enforcer reviews the deal.

The so-called second request by the FTC signals that the agency is reviewing whether the transaction could be anticompetitive under U.S. law.

Gallagher, which in December agreed to buy AssuredPartners from private-equity firms GTCR and Apax Partners, said it is actively responding to FTC request, and that it now expects to complete the acquisition in the second half of the year.

The Rolling Meadows, Ill., insurance brokerage, risk-management and consulting-services had previously said it expected to close the deal by the end of March.

The FTC become more active under the Biden administration in moving against large mergers, but Andrew Ferguson. the agency's new chairman appointed by President Trump, is widely expected to go easier on deals.

The FTC on Thursday sued to block the $627 million takeover of medical-device company Surmodics by GTCR, claiming the deal would create a company that controls more than half of the market for outsourced hydrophilic coatings, which are used in medical devices such as catheters and guidewires.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com

data.title: Dow Opens Lower; Jobs Report Slightly Misses Forecasts — WSJ
data.description:
By Chelsey Dulaney

Investors are digging into the jobs report as a tumultuous week for global markets nears its end.

The widely eyed report showed hiring picked up a touch in February, with 151,000 jobs added, but slightly undershot expectations for growth of 170,000. The unemployment rate crept up to 4.1%, while it had been forecast to hold steady.

Stock futures wavered, with indexes on track for weekly losses, after markets took another battering Thursday.

Investors had been braced for a big stock-market swing after the release, options-trading data suggested.

The jobs report comes as investors fret over the U.S. economic outlook. The Trump administration's swerving trade policy is weighing on growth expectations and consumer and business confidence, survey data suggests. The latest U-turn came Thursday, when the White House granted a tariff reprieve on some imports from Mexico and Canada until April 2.

In recent trading:

Stock futures were close to flat, giving up earlier gains. On Thursday, the S&P 500 lost 1.8%, closing at its lowest level since Nov. 4, the day before President Trump's election. The Nasdaq Composite fell 2.6% to end in correction territory.

Chip stocks rebounded. Broadcom's upbeat results sent its stock up 12% before the bell. Other semiconductor stocks such as Nvidia and ASML also climbed.

U.S. Treasury yields moved lower, as the global bond rout eased. European bond yields were also mostly lower, after a historic selloff this week.

The greenback weakened further. The WSJ Dollar Index dropped to its lowest level since the days after President Trump's election in November. The euro strengthened toward $1.09.

The European stock rally paused. Benchmarks retreated about 1% in Germany and France. They are still on track for weekly gains, after a surge in European defense stocks.

Coming up:

Federal Reserve officials, including Jerome Powell, are set to make their last public remarks before the central bank's next rate decision. The Fed chair is due to discuss the economic outlook at 12:30 p.m. ET.

Write to Chelsey Dulaney at chelsey.dulaney@wsj.com
